nativeRange.habitat: The Empire Gudgeon inhabits the lower reaches of flowing streams, rivers, and creeks across its range, and tolerates brackish estuaries and occasionally full seawater salinity. Juveniles tend to occur in swiftly flowing water and brackish zones, while adults are more commonly found in freshwater among aquatic vegetation and submerged timber.

decorPreferences: dark sandy or fine gravel substrate
flat rocks and smooth stones for territory and spawning surfaces
driftwood for cover and territory division
hardy plants (Java fern, Anubias, water sprite) as visual breaks
dense vegetation along sides and back for weaker individuals to retreat
open sandy areas in foreground for normal resting behaviour

diet.notes: The Empire Gudgeon is primarily carnivorous, feeding on small invertebrates in the wild, including micro-crustaceans, mosquito larvae, and other small aquatic invertebrates. In the aquarium it generally accepts small live and frozen foods such as bloodworm (chironomid larvae), mosquito larvae, brine shrimp, Daphnia, Mysis, and tubifex. Quality dried micro-pellets and flake are also taken once the fish is established. The species is predatory toward any fish small enough to fit in its mouth, so tank mate selection should account for the fish's gape size.

goodWith: similarly sized Australian natives (rainbow fish, blue-eye rainbowfish)
medium-bodied tetras and barbs too large to be eaten
bristlenose plecos and other robust bottom dwellers
larger gudgeon species of similar size
avoidWith: small fish (neon tetras, nano rasboras, small livebearers) — will be eaten
dwarf shrimp and small invertebrates — high predation risk
very long-finned or slow fish that may be harassed by territorial males
other bottom-dwelling territorial species competing for the same zone

imageCandidates.notes: Frank M. Greco; 500x299. Adult male in full nuptial colour showing the diagnostic vivid red-orange dorsal and anal fins with black margins and electric-blue edging. Sharp, whole fish, well-lit. Modest resolution but by far the most striking and correctly ID'd image.

Tom Ferguson; 2048x1536. Two subadults in a survey container showing the reddish cheek patch and yellow body; correctly ID'd, in focus. Muted/juvenile colour so kept only as a non-recommended secondary showing multiple individuals.

summary: The Empire Gudgeon (Hypseleotris compressa) is a small Australian sleeper goby reaching 12 cm. Males develop vivid red-orange, yellow, and blue fin coloration at breeding condition. Captive breeding is essentially impossible: larvae must reach saltwater to survive, a journey that cannot be replicated in the home aquarium.
careNotes:
The Empire Gudgeon is a mid-sized sleeper goby (family Eleotridae) native to freshwater rivers and streams of northern and eastern Australia and southern New Guinea, with a natural range extending from the Pilbara and Kimberley regions of Western Australia coastally to New South Wales. In the wild it inhabits the lower reaches of rivers and creeks and is tolerant of brackish estuarine conditions and even full seawater salinity, though aquarium keeping is conducted in freshwater or very mildly brackish conditions.

Males are dramatically sexually dimorphic at breeding condition, developing vivid red-orange, yellow, and blue patterning on the dorsal and anal fins — among the most colourful of Australian freshwater natives. Females and juveniles show muted brown-grey patterning. A group of 6 or more (ideally several females per male) is recommended to display natural behaviour and distribute territorial aggression. Males are territorial with one another and will compete vigorously; tank length of at least 90 cm and dense cover are essential to allow subordinate fish to escape dominant individuals.

The Empire Gudgeon is broadly carnivorous and predatory: it will consume any fish small enough to fit in its mouth. Tank mates must be chosen with the fish’s gape size in mind. Feeding on small live or frozen invertebrate foods (bloodworm, Artemia, Daphnia, mosquito larvae) is recommended; dried foods are accepted once the fish is established but live or frozen items should form the majority of the diet.

The species is cool-tolerant, reflecting the strong seasonal temperature variation of its native Australian range; it can withstand temperatures as low as 10–15°C in cooler months, making it a viable choice for unheated subtropical setups in temperate climates. Optimal aquarium temperatures of 20–26°C are broadly recommended. Water chemistry is flexible across a wide pH and hardness range, but extremes should be avoided. Standard filtration providing four to five times hourly turnover is appropriate; strong currents are not required.
breedingNotes: Breeding in the home aquarium is not achieved under normal circumstances. The Empire Gudgeon has an amphidromous (diadromous) reproductive strategy: adults spawn in freshwater, but the extremely small post-hatch larvae (approximately 1 mm, with incompletely developed digestive tracts at hatching) are carried downstream by river currents into estuarine and fully marine environments, where they complete early development before migrating back to freshwater as juvenile fish. Recreating this marine larval phase in captivity is impractical for the hobbyist. Males establish territories, display to females, and can be induced to spawn over flat rock surfaces or sandy substrate in aquaria; females may deposit up to ~3,000 adhesive eggs which the male guards and fans. However, hatching larvae require immediate access to marine conditions and appropriately sized live marine microplankton, making successful rearing essentially impossible without specialist facilities. The species is consequently almost never bred in captivity and is sold as wild-caught or farm-raised stock from Australia.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Hypseleotris compressa
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (100–120 mm SL), temperature (20–35°C), pH (5.0–9.1), hardness (36–447 ppm), minimum tank base (80×30 cm, ~72 L), diet (small live/frozen invertebrates), native range (northern and eastern Australia, southern New Guinea), amphidromous lifecycle notes, and captive breeding difficulty. Temperature lower bound adjusted to 15°C per broader tolerance data (FishBase cites 10°C).

additional.name: FishBase — Hypseleotris compressa
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (max 12.0 cm SL, common 5 cm), temperature (10–30°C), pH (5.0–9.1), native range (Australia and southern central New Guinea), taxonomy (Eleotridae, Gobiiformes), diet (micro-crustaceans, mosquito larvae), habitat (lower river reaches, brackish tolerance). Clutch size up to 3,000 eggs; eggs among smallest vertebrate eggs (~0.32 mm). Hatching in 10–14 hours noted.

additional.name: Aquadiction — Empire Gudgeon Species Spotlight
additional.notes: Provided gH range (2–25), pH (5.0–8.0), temperature (20–35°C), lifespan (up to 5 years), sexual dimorphism details (male vivid multicoloured dorsal/anal fins), minimum group of 6, tank decor preferences. Confirmed carnivorous diet preference for live/frozen invertebrates. Breeding described as rare accomplishment requiring amphidromous larval marine phase.

additional.name: Aqua-Fish.net — Empire Gudgeon
additional.notes: Secondary cross-check. Confirmed amphidromous lifecycle, tank size (~80 L minimum), temperature (20–27°C for aquarium), pH 6.5–7.4 for aquarium keeping, sandy substrate preference, and predatory nature toward small tank mates. Lifespan 5–8 years cited.

fish.breedingNotes: The Empire Gudgeon is an amphidromous species — adults spawn in freshwater, but the tiny post-hatch larvae (approximately 1 mm) hatch with incompletely developed digestive tracts and must immediately reach fully marine conditions to survive and develop. Males guard adhesive egg clutches of up to around 3,000 eggs deposited on flat rock or substrate. The marine larval rearing phase is impractical in the home aquarium, making captive breeding essentially impossible. Stock in the trade is wild-caught or farm-raised.
